title: Exodus 7
subtitle: Aaron's Staff; Plague One — Water to Blood

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — Aaron as Moses’ Prophet; Their Roles Before Pharaoh

heb.paragraph: V.1: "I have made you like God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron will be your prophet (nābîʾ)." This is the earliest definition of prophetic function in the OT: the prophet speaks what the one with authority has given him to speak. Aaron's role anticipates all prophetic vocation — the prophet is the mouthpiece of a higher word, not the originator of his own message.

V.3: "I will harden Pharaoh's heart (ḥizzaqtî ʾet-leb parʿōh)." The verb ḥāzaq (to be strong, firm, hard) is used differently in different plague texts — sometimes God hardens (chs 7-10), sometimes Pharaoh hardens himself (chs 8-9). The theological point: Pharaoh's own resistance reveals his character while simultaneously serving God's purposes for multiplying signs and witnesses (Exod 9:16; Rom 9:17).

panels.hist: Egyptian magicians' ability to manipulate serpents is documented — a text from the First Intermediate Period describes a magician handling serpents. The uraeus cobra was the symbol of pharaonic power and divine protection. The staff-to-serpent sign directly confronts Egypt's most potent religious symbol: YHWH's serpent swallows Pharaoh's serpent.
panels.ctx: The framework speech (vv.1–7) is crucial for reading all ten plagues. Three elements: (1) purpose — "the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD"; (2) mechanism — the hardening; (3) scope — "great acts of judgment." The plagues are not primarily punishments but revelations. Each plague targets a domain Egypt claims is controlled by one of its gods — YHWH systematically dismantles the Egyptian theological worldview domain by domain.

cross.note: Paul uses the hardening of Pharaoh's heart to discuss divine sovereignty and human responsibility — both are real; neither cancels the other.
The seven bowl judgments echo the ten plagues — blood (bowl 2), darkness (bowl 5), etc. Exodus's plague sequence is the typological template for eschatological judgment.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes the designation of Moses as 'elohim to Pharaoh' is unique in the OT. Elohim is used here in its sense of 'divine being' or 'one with divine authority' — Moses is the representative of the divine in the confrontation with Pharaoh. The phrasing inverts Pharaoh's claim to divine status: the one Pharaoh claims to be (god-king), Moses actually is — not inherently, but by divine appointment for this encounter.

The Egyptian magicians who replicate Moses' signs are called chakhamim (wise men) and mekhashfim (sorcerers/enchanters). Sarna notes that Egyptian religious texts describe elaborate ritual magic involving serpent transformation. The Torah does not dismiss Egyptian magic as illusion; it acknowledges its reality while demonstrating its subordination to the power of YHWH. The theological claim is not that Egyptian magic is fake but that it is outmatched.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The purpose statement frames the plague narrative as a demonstration project. Alter notes the three-part audience: Pharaoh (who will not listen), Egypt (who will know), and implicitly Israel (who must witness). The plagues are simultaneously a judgment on Egypt, a liberation of Israel, and a revelation of divine character to all. The multiple purposes explain why the process is prolonged rather than immediate — the demonstration must be complete.

Aaron's rod swallowing the Egyptian rods is a small but pointed victory — a preview of what the full plague sequence will accomplish. The narrative presents it without commentary; the swallowing speaks for itself. The image is structurally precise: just as the staff consumes the staffs, the Exodus will consume Egypt's power over Israel. The sign is proleptic.

Calvin on Moses as 'god to Pharaoh': 'Moses is called the god of Pharaoh because he is the interpreter and messenger of the Most High God, and is armed with his power.' The role is functional, not ontological — Moses represents God's authority in this specific confrontation. Calvin extends the principle: the minister of God's word always brings divine authority to bear, not personal authority.

Calvin on the magicians: 'I have no doubt that Satan aided them to perform these things.' He takes the magicians' power seriously — it is real, but demonic. The theological point is that demonic power is genuine but limited; it can imitate but cannot surpass divine creation. When Aaron's rod swallows the magicians' rods, the limit of counterfeit power is publicly demonstrated.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'harden' (qasheh, 'make hard/stiff') here is a third hardening verb (the others being chazaq and kabed). The note explains all three convey related ideas of resolute refusal — the variation in vocabulary reflects the richness of Hebrew idiom rather than theological difference. The accumulation of hardening language across the plague narrative underscores the total, comprehensive nature of Pharaoh's resistance.

The NET note on the Egyptian magicians references the tradition preserved in 2 Tim 3:8 that names them Jannes and Jambres. These names appear in post-biblical Jewish sources (Dead Sea Scrolls, Targum Jonathan) and in Paul's letter. The note discusses whether the names were known from lost sources or represent later legendary development.

MacArthur: I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD. The purpose of the plagues is dual: Israel's deliverance and Egypt's instruction. The multiplied signs proclaim YHWH's identity to the watching world.
MacArthur: Aaron's staff swallowed the magicians' staffs. Yet Pharaoh's heart was hardened. The sign was given and rejected. Hardening is not the absence of evidence but the refusal of evidence.

sections.header: Verses 10—25 — The First Plague: Water Turned to Blood

heb.paragraph: V.9-12: Aaron's staff becomes a tannîn — a serpent or sea-creature (the same word used for sea monsters in Gen 1:21; Ps 74:13; Isa 27:1). When Aaron's serpent swallows the Egyptian magicians' serpents, it signals YHWH's sovereignty over chaos and the forces Egypt's magic tried to manipulate. Egypt's power is not matched but consumed.

The Egyptian magicians who replicate Moses and Aaron's signs are ḥarṭummîm — likely lector-priests with access to magical texts. They succeed with the first two signs (staff to serpent, blood, frogs) but fail at the gnats (v.18). Their failure at the third plague marks the tipping point: "This is the finger of God." Egyptian sacred power is exposed as finite before YHWH's infinite authority.

panels.hist: The Nile turning to blood (the first plague) directly attacks Hapi, the Egyptian god of the Nile flood. The annual inundation was the basis of Egypt's agricultural prosperity and was celebrated in religious festivals. A natural phenomenon of red-coloured algal bloom (Oscillatoria rubescens) in the Nile has been proposed as a naturalistic backdrop, though the text presents this as a supernatural act. The Egyptian magicians' ability to replicate the sign follows the pattern of magical contest known from Egyptian literature.
panels.ctx: The first plague targets the Nile — Egypt's most sacred resource and the source of its agricultural power. The Egyptian gods of the Nile (Hapi, Osiris) cannot protect their domain. Fish die; water is undrinkable for seven days (v.25). The magicians' ability to replicate the plague (v.22) does not diminish YHWH — they cannot reverse it, only add to the problem. Pharaoh's response (v.23: "he did not take even this to heart") sets the pattern for the entire plague sequence: each judgment is met with hardened refusal.

cross.note: The psalmist recalls the first plague: 'He turned their rivers into blood; they could not drink from their streams.' The plagues tradition was liturgically rehearsed as evidence of God's sovereign power.
The second and third bowl judgments turn the sea and rivers to blood, echoing this plague. Revelation's final judgment recapitulates Egypt's — the exodus pattern shapes eschatology.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The first plague's theological target is unmistakable. Sarna notes that Hapi, the god of the Nile inundation, was one of the most important deities in the Egyptian pantheon. The annual Nile flood was worshipped as the source of agricultural life. Turning the Nile to blood was not merely a natural catastrophe but a theological statement: the god of Egypt's life-source cannot protect its own domain.

The Egyptians digging around the Nile for water demonstrates two things: the plague's severity (the river itself was undrinkable) and human ingenuity under divine judgment (they found groundwater). Sarna notes this small detail grounds the narrative in realistic detail — the plague did not make all water unavailable, just the Nile itself. The suffering was severe but not immediately lethal.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The plague's execution is narrated with deliberate economy: Moses does as commanded, the Nile turns to blood, the fish die, the water stinks, the Egyptians cannot drink. Five consequences in rapid succession. Alter notes the pace — no pause, no Pharaoh's reaction, no dialogue — until the single sentence about the magicians (v.22). The brevity of the execution matches the definitiveness of the act.

The magicians' ability to replicate the first plague — turning water to blood — is the narrative's first indication that they can duplicate but not reverse divine signs. Alter notes the Pharaoh-hardens pattern: each plague that the magicians replicate allows Pharaoh to dismiss the sign as non-unique. The replication is a mercy that becomes a hardness.

Calvin on the first plague: 'God chose the Nile rather than any other water because it was held in the greatest veneration by the Egyptians. He signified thereby that the most holy things of Egypt were the objects of his wrath.' The theological targeting is precise: the sacred is judged first. The Nile's sanctity made its defilement the most powerful possible statement of divine superiority over Egyptian religion.

Calvin: The magicians replicated the miracle not to vindicate Moses but to harden Pharaoh's heart. God permitted this satanic replication to demonstrate that he would vindicate his servant through a power that no human imitation could match. The greater the opposition, the greater the vindication.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'all their pools of water' (mikvehem) discusses the geography of Egyptian water storage: the Nile, its canals, the pools (reservoirs), and vessels of wood and stone. The scope of the plague is comprehensive — not just the main river but all water storage. The note also discusses the magicians' replication: if all water was already blood, what water did the magicians use? The text may imply they found water not yet affected, or the replication was symbolic rather than large-scale.

The NET note on the seven-day duration after the plague discusses the theological significance: the Nile was sacred to Egypt. Converting it to blood for seven full days was not a single act but a sustained declaration that YHWH's power over the sacred river was complete and prolonged.

MacArthur: The Nile turned to blood -- the river Egypt worshipped as the god Hapi was turned into death. The first plague targets the source of Egypt's life and the object of its worship simultaneously. Every plague has a target in the Egyptian pantheon.

MacArthur: The blood of the Nile strikes Egypt's most sacred geographic feature. The Nile was worshipped as Hapy, the god of flood and fertility. Turning it to blood was a theological confrontation before it was an environmental judgment. YHWH's first blow lands on what Egypt considered most divine.

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: Pharaoh is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: Three elements: (1) purpose — "the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD"…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"The Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch out my hand against Egypt and bring out the people of Israel from among them."NIV"And the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD when I stretch out my hand against Egypt and bring the Israelites out of it."

src.title: Ipuwer Papyrus
src.quote: Egyptian lamentation text describing national catastrophe including a river of blood, widespread death, and social collapse. Disputed dating (c.1850 or 1650 BC).
src.note: Whether or not directly related to the Exodus plagues, it demonstrates Egypt's literary capacity to describe such catastrophe.

src.title: Egyptian Serpent Cult
src.quote: The uraeus cobra adorned Pharaoh's crown as the divine protector. Snake-handling by Egyptian priests is documented in magical papyri.
src.note: Aaron's serpent swallowing Pharaoh's is a deliberate theological challenge to Egyptian royal theology.

rec.text: The second and third bowl judgments turn sea and rivers to blood — the first plague pattern applied to eschatological judgment.
The serpent that swallows the Egyptian serpents typifies Christ's cross swallowing the power of death — "death is swallowed up in victory" (1 Cor 15:54).

rows.label: Blood: The First Plague and the Sacred River
rows.text: The Nile -- Egypt's sacred river, source of life, god-identified -- becomes blood. The first plague targets the centre of Egyptian religious geography. YHWH begins with what Egypt worships most.

rows.label: The Magicians Replicate but Cannot Reverse
rows.text: The court magicians can match the sign but cannot undo it. The ability to replicate is not the same as the ability to reverse. Pharaoh's hardening is enabled by the partial competence of his own court.

hebtext.note: V.1: "I have made you like God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron will be your prophet (nābîʾ)." This is the earliest definition of prophetic function in the OT: the prophet speaks what the one with authority has given him to speak. The verb ḥāzaq (to be strong, firm, hard) is used differently in different plague texts — sometimes God hardens (chs 7-10), sometimes Pharaoh hardens himself (chs 8-9). The theological point: Pharaoh's own resistance reveals his character while simultaneously serving God's purposes for multiplying signs and witnesses (Exod 9:16; Rom 9:17).

V.9-12: Aaron's staff becomes a tannîn — a serpent or sea-creature (the same word used for sea monsters in Gen 1:21; Ps 74:13; Isa 27:1). When Aaron's serpent swallows the Egyptian magicians' serpents, it signals YHWH's sovereignty over chaos and the forces Egypt's magic tried to manipulate. Egypt's power is not matched but consumed.

Egyptian sacred power is exposed as finite before YHWH's infinite authority.

The seven bowl judgments echo the ten plagues — blood (bowl 2), darkness (bowl 5), etc. Exodus's plague sequence is the typological template for eschatological judgment.

tx.title: Moses as 'god to Pharaoh'
tx.content: MT reads 'I have made you a god (ʾĕlōhîm) to Pharaoh.' The LXX softens to θεόν ('a god'), but some LXX manuscripts add 'as if a god.' Targum Onqelos substantially rewrites: 'I have appointed you as a master (rab) to Pharaoh,' avoiding the theological difficulty entirely.
tx.note: The Targum's 'master' for ʾĕlōhîm reflects the strong anti-anthropomorphic tendency in Onqelos. The MT's bold metaphor is the harder reading and almost certainly original.

debate.title: Natural vs. Supernatural: The Nature of the Plagues

positions.name: Purely supernatural
positions.argument: Each plague is a direct divine act with no natural mechanism. The timing, specificity, and discrimination (Goshen spared in plagues 4–10) require direct divine causation. The plagues are signs precisely because they transcend natural explanation.

positions.name: Naturalistic cascade
positions.argument: Hort proposed that each plague follows naturally from the previous: unusual Nile algae (blood-red) → frog exodus → dying frogs → flies and disease → etc. The sequence is climatologically plausible for Egypt. Divine causation operates through natural processes rather than suspending them.

positions.name: Theological signs with natural elements
positions.argument: The plagues may involve natural phenomena divinely intensified and directed. The theological significance is in the timing, the discrimination between Egypt and Goshen, and the interpretation provided by Moses. God works through nature without being limited to its normal patterns.
